title: Class decorators

glossary.definition: Standard pieces of code that have to be written over and over again with little alteration.
To change or alter the internal state or data of an object.

overview.what: A class decorator is a function that takes a class object as an argument and returns a modified class. Instead of modifying instances, it alters the class definition itself, such as adding properties, methods, or registering the class. Think of it like a car factory adding a custom spoiler to every car model before any cars are actually produced.
overview.why: It reduces boilerplate when you need to apply the same structural changes (like adding standard methods or registering to a central store) across many classes.

why_learning_this: Understand how @dataclass magically adds __init__ and __repr__ to your classes
Learn to register classes automatically upon definition

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting to return the class
common_mistakes.explanation: If your class decorator doesn't return the class object, the class name will be bound to None (or whatever you returned).

common_mistakes.title: Mutating the original class unexpectedly
common_mistakes.explanation: Class decorators usually mutate the class in-place and return it. If multiple parts of the codebase expect the unmodified class, this can cause hidden bugs.

recall_questions.q: What argument does a class decorator function receive?
recall_questions.answer: It receives the class object itself, not an instance of the class.

recall_questions.q: Why would you use a class decorator instead of standard inheritance?
recall_questions.answer: Class decorators are useful for applying standard modifications (like adding boilerplate methods or registering the class) without enforcing a rigid inheritance hierarchy.

practice_tasks.title: Modify Plugin Registration
practice_tasks.prompt: The `register_plugin` decorator currently appends the class name to `PLUGINS`. Modify it so that it appends the actual class object to `PLUGINS` instead, and ensure it returns the class.

code_walkthrough.focus: cls.id_field = 'default_id' # The class object is mutated directly

aha_moment.prediction: What is the type of App?
aha_moment.why: Because `oops_decorator` didn't return anything, it implicitly returned `None`. The name `App` is now bound to `None`, not the class.

challenge.title: Adding a class-level timestamp
challenge.prompt: Create a class decorator `add_timestamp` that adds a class attribute `created_at` to the decorated class. Set its value to `'2023-01-01'`. Apply this decorator to a class `Record` and print `Record.created_at`.

understanding_checks.question: What does this code print?
understanding_checks.why: The class decorator receives the class object, modifies its `value` attribute directly to 20, and returns it.

understanding_checks.question: Why would you choose a class decorator over modifying instances directly in the `__init__` method?
understanding_checks.answer: Class decorators alter the class definition itself once (e.g., adding standard boilerplate or registering the class globally), rather than running logic every time a new instance is created.
understanding_checks.why: This reinforces the mental model that a class decorator modifies the blueprint, not the individual objects produced from it.
